title: Why Great Injectors Don't Have To Be Good At Art...Yet! [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: Why Great Injectors Don_t Have To Be Good At Art...Yet! [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: Welcome to the aesthetics mastery show don't use that one on them welcome to the aesthetics mastery show the show that helps you raise the bar and thrive in your aesthetics career i'm dr.

segments.text: welcome to the aesthetics mastery show don't use that one on them welcome to the aesthetics mastery show the show that helps you raise the bar and thrive in your aesthetics career i'm dr. sim PS i'm miranda pierce and what are we talking about today manga so you might have noticed that we have something in between us there's another woman in our relationship yes there's definitely another woman in our relationship she was born last week so I'd only call her Teresa some reason it's named her right here and this is a sculpture that Tim produced last week which we did for different reasons but you learn an amazing amount on the road particularly about aesthetics in aesthetics so what do I mean by that so I don't know if any of you have really broken it down I have

to do this as an educator really break down what it is that we need to be very good at in aesthetics and often it's anatomy an injection technique and there's a lot of stuff around safety which I'm really passionate about I love learning and preventing complications in particular and often the medical models means we start from that position but ultimately we only need to know all of that stuff so that we don't injure people the real thing we're trying to do is make people more beautiful and that's a different framework and actually the deeper thing I believe is the real thing we're doing is making people happier so its well-being ultimately so the model of anatomy then aesthetics and then psychology and is how I like to operate we need to know the anatomy so that we can create beauty so that we can make people happier and that's the sequence we need to master there's not a huge amount on creating beauty I see most injection techniques as isolated components of the face like how do you rotate a lip and show more lip and you know all these different techniques but

they're not there's not a lot on understanding the whole beauty of the face and how it all works together and nothing will teach you that better than trying to create a face from scratch and seeing if you can make a beautiful face and it's not easy and you won't enjoy it the first time because it's frustrating but in that frustration lies one of the best learning experiences that you can get okay so just before we move on to talk about your experience sculpting I feel like there's something that needs mentioning which is that I see people practitioners resisting almost this idea of using the word beautiful with their patients and the reason for that is they don't want to say you aren't beautiful and they're very comfortable to say oh yes you've aged because this is what happens with the faces at Ages and it's kind of it feels we're all comfortable with that but to then say oh I'm gonna make you look fresher and younger but also I'm gonna make you look more beautiful especially it's the customer obviously does often doesn't say that themselves but that is what we are in the pursuit

of I think you have to get comfortable with that don't we if we're gonna really create those amazing transformations when I look at the before in artists that you do when you've done you know 15 plus syringes with someone and you can barely even understand what's going on with it because it's so subtle and spread across the face that's beauty and that's not looking done it's the opposite of some of the trends you know the crazy squared your lines and the massive lips and whatever so I think we have to have that conversation even if it's just with ourselves yeah I mean you are you're touching on one of the most difficult problems which is if you say that you value beauty you're also saying that you don't value things that are less beautiful and that's where it gets very uncomfortable and I think the flip side of it is right back where I started which is the psychology which is essentially for one of a better word the most sacred thing that there is someone's consciousness and you want each person to feel on the inside valued and appreciated no matter where they are on all the different value structures we

create because being human should be innately and basically sacred for one of a better word so we need to be able to respect individuals for who they are but also respect them enough that if they say I will be happier if you can make me more you more beautiful that you can also do that for them without saying that you value the individual less who isn't beautiful but you we'll have to value beauty so it's almost like separating the individual from beauty itself like we have to we have to acknowledge that it's built into us anyone who says oh you know everyone's the same and I don't I treat everyone the same I mean what they mean is they value each individual but we are animals and in terms of how we experience things and when we see someone is beautiful if you don't respond to that you're ultimately not equipped to survive because beauty is a signal for health that's basically what it is and if you're not able to recognize healthy members of your species and react on a physiological level to them you're basically doomed like you can't have a species that operates that way so beauty is about survival it doesn't mean they're philosophically we can't

valued in all individuals the same and it's hard because I think what practitioners are trying not to do is get involved the same you know you're less beautiful and therefore less valuable I think you can separate the two out I think you can be as valuable as the next person without being as beautiful as the next person so tell me about your process with Teresa I I'd highly recommend that you brave enough to try it now I know it's a bit scary I was a bit scared to start I'm the first sculpture that I ever did which i think is behind here actually so this is one of the first ones I did it's actually the second one but I didn't get much of pictures under the wrist but this is supposed to be a woman but my little boy said to me who's that man so Chin's a bit wide so expect your first one to be a bit crap and if you try and there are lots of reasons for that took me ages and nowhere near as good as that one but that's the process that you go through as you start out with something that you don't like and this is that the great thing about sculpting is you have to pull all your theoretical

knowledge and your experience into one because you know that the chin for example is the same width as a nose in a female and you can literally create that you know what a jawline looks like in two dimensions but then you've got to try and create it in three dimensions which we all know from injecting that's actually one of the hardest procedures if you look at the face and isolated components so you're in this battle between applying what you know intellectually and then actually experiencing your result and then there's conflict because you you've done what you thought you needed to do with your proportion but you look at it and it's like that doesn't look pretty at all I do not find myself attracted to that [ __ ] and you're trying to create something as beautiful I remember thinking as I was making this I need to make something I'm not gonna stop until I actually feel like if that was a real person I think they would actually be quite attractive and that's a hard challenge because it because it's actually it's actually very hard and most of the time it looks weird I use the word weird as a good is a good example because we these subtle shapes and curvatures are so

difficult to get right that all you sense is a sense of weirdness you're not even sure it's wrong within now that frustration you should not resent that frustration I always say this one on training objectives particularly people are getting to the top end the doctors who work for us a really great injectors they're all artistic but they also just like me feel frustrated at times I like why is this how can I not get it better and I would say value that frustration because within that frustration is what you're basically saying when you're frustrated is I know what beauty is I just can't control this material to bring it out and that's the same as we face with injecting so what you need to do is embrace that and just stare at it I found myself just staring it at this trying to think what is it that's wrong right looking each part of the face looking the relationships between the face looking at symmetry and something will suddenly jump out at you no girl that's what it is okay the lateral canthus is slightly lower than it should be for a female which was one of my learning points I know that

intellectually not until I was staring at her face thinking why is she not attractive but you know 15 minutes and then I suddenly realized she needs to have their lateral canthus lifted then I do it and then you see it because then it's the junk it's the combination of the battle of frustration and then the feeling you get and you get this with patience too when you do a great job which is you suddenly look at them and they actually make you feel differently it's one thing getting rid of a wrinkle it's a whole different thing looking at someone and basically getting a bit of a zing that they didn't give you when they first walked in that's an amazing thing you can do that on people but you can magnify your ability your abilities to do that by going through the battle of trying to sculpt from scratch so that's the first thing is I'd like people to not be frustrated with when they're frustrated because the is it's there too it's telling you that you know more than you're capable of at the moment it's like you know when I say no I mean in the deepest possible way like you're the software and your mind the there's been there for millennia that's running a small hardware actually knows more than your conscious mind is

electric yet grasp but that's great because it's there the people I worry about other people who can't even see what's wrong with it so if you're putting out before and afters that are basically worse and you can't see it those people are stuck there's not a lot I can do for them but if they're frustrated I can help them if it'd been me and I had seen this on the YouTube show and I thought oh wow Tim's done this sculpture probably what I would think is Tim's good at sculpting you know Tim's an aesthetics master you know of course he's gonna sculpt well I'm rubbish I wouldn't Scott well and I think actually when it comes to art loads of us have this limiting belief that with terrible art like the same with tech or money or you know you could say you know I'm a tech foe or you know I'm terrible with Madison and that's one of the things that we say about arts so easy within us and if it is then is that mean discovered if the key element is that you can tell when it's bad okay the fact that you created it and it's bad is almost irrelevant what matters most is that you can see

it's bad if you can see it's bad there's hope okay if you're looking at it and you think it's amazing and it's awful then you're stuck so is what I'm saying is people need to appreciate that when they look at something and of that they've created and think oh my god I hope no one ever sees that that's that means there's hope because what I know what'll happen cuz I've been through this I had with this sculpture moments of thinking I don't want anyone to ever see this is embarrassing and you know I'm never gonna be able to do it when I was doing the eyes I mean they just looked like gremlins for ages so but in the process of battling it like if you commit to delving into what's wrong with it and trying again that's what learning is all about that's I know how long that's four years old that I really gosh so I was showing Jasper this and Jasper said who's that man has not meant to be a man has meant to be a woman but and you can see she's she as a flat face a right-angle on her nose a broad chin so it actually does look more like a man but it was supposed to be a woman and

there's all sorts of things that are clearly not right about that I mean it's okay because I was obviously as happy enough to put it on a stick and keep it but she's knowing nowhere near as good as this one and that's iteration there was another one in between this is the next one and this one is better obviously we've got no ears but also alien-looking like even without even if you stick hair on it it's still a bit alien looking look how strong the chin is a bit too strong nose is disproportionately small so there are things I don't like about it but it was you know as good step in the right direction that would be my the key thing is to acknowledge that you're frustrated and then make it better and look out for the moments of Epiphany because well that's what happens is you're staring at it you hate it Youth you don't know what is wrong with it you try something and suddenly it looks better and sometimes you're not even and there that's the moment where your subconscious and your conscious are connecting you're suddenly saying I know what beauty is I'm not creating it and then it moves there and then you discover that that's slight doubt that

slight curvature at this point or a slight change in the angle of the lateral canthus which is one of the points I had going on with her makes it suddenly better and I'm not happy with completely happy with her either but there's that great saying which is great art is never finished it's abandoned so I've had to abandon her and I'll try again and do something different and try and make it slightly better there are things I would do differently how to do it I was to do it again and progress so how did you do it so you need to what I discovered as I was doing this is that artists when you could have done some online courses with it a lot of artists I was really thrilled to find out actually start by sculpting the anatomy first so they create a skull which is why I did then they lay over the muscles and the fact the deep fat pads and then the superficial fat pads and what they're actually doing is they're acknowledging that the nuances of the human face are so subtle that if you don't build it from the ground up it won't look quite right so they're actually literally building each muscle oh it's weird to watch because they lay

down you know you're depressor angular asaurus and then they blend in you can't even see what they put but that what they're saying is the volume the proportion there is significant enough that if I really want a natural a really natural likeness I need to build this from the ground up and they add each thing on its own and then blend it in and it really works wow that's so amazing I love that and it just proves the point that art is close to the science of aesthetics yeah well it's really interesting to think of it as the as I think I said might've said previously or on a different take and that the that we often think of Anatomy as something you need to understand in order to be safe but actually it's the foundations of beauty as well and when you're aware of you can look at someone's face and say the reason you're not as beautiful as you could be is because I can't see the line that your zygomatic major should create on this part of your face and I can maybe reveal more of that with a little bit of volume in your cheek you that kind of deeper understanding will enable you to create

really beautiful results and but it's really fun as well like if you can you'll know your Anatomy loads better if you decide to do something like this but you also you see that link really powerful so you said you made a skull so what's going on underneath there yeah so I copied one of these I basically copied a plastic skull initially so I started I got the layout and the you know the cheekbones in particular and the jaw and the angles of the nose created all of that first and actually one of the first steps up and say is do the profile and until that looked right I didn't continue and then you start to layer on top of that the other proportions but it's actually really similar to how you would do a good full face restoration in that you start with the structure you don't start getting rid of lines and wrinkles you start replacing sure that you can see the angle and the zygoma and the you know the angle on your chin - not everyone injects this way but it's basically how I naturally inject is I

first create the focal point so the focal point is often where multiple lines are meeting so there is something I learned also from sculpture which is they're these facial planes and if you create the facial plane where the planes meet is one of the focal points and actually for years I've been in acting by creating the focal point first and then making sure that the connecting points are there also connecting planes between the focal points you make sure those are nice those flow nicely and then once you've got that structure correct you start to add the detail in at the end and that's exactly the same process as you do with sculpting make that real what do you mean by focal like beavers example on the face so focal points and you've got the brow Ridge here so there's a the frontal surface the temple surface and then the orbital surface and they meet at one particular point that's a really important point of beauty under spoken about actually and this point above your eye you've got the same thing going on with your cheek the angle on the zygoma a little scratch on there angle on her zygoma and then tapering off

you've got the connecting line similar with the chin so Chin's another focal point there's two focal points on the chin and lips offs have got multiple little focal points in them but it's the same principle nose as well as got a focal point tip of your nose you've got the spine of your nose these are the bits that your brain recognizes as a human face I give those focal points aren't in the right place then it doesn't know what your face is essentially if you think about your brain is a pattern recognizing piece of machinery like that's what it does it recognizes patterns and it was why if there's ever a shadow or cloud we often see faces in clouds it's because your brain is taking all that information and trying to force it into something it means something it's demeaning well you see Jesus in a piece of toast yeah absolutely and think it's the same it's your software going what does this pattern mean and the most likely thing it will it'll want to see as other human faces human Vaes are the most important thing to us so we're always looking for those structures that represent human faces and then more than just human faces it's

beautiful human faces that we're looking for yeah it's funny I'm not a clinician myself but sometimes I'll notice someone on TV like a man who looks a little bit like a woman and I'll say to you like why does he look like a woman and I don't know but I do know I know it's going on yeah I don't know why so that that's the ingredient to be in an artist is that you or in a set a good aesthetic clinician is that you battle with that until you know the answer because you do know the answer you just don't know it with your cerebral cortex you know with the deeper part of your and as soon as you make the connection you now have the power to control it and this is what we're always in the battle to achieve which is the aesthetic clinician needs to know how to control aesthetics and to control aesthetics you need to have conscious control of what it is the beauty is if you're trying to make someone beautiful but you're not you're just injecting where the point is that you learned on the diagram and that's a very different thing to actually feeling in your bones and then making it conscious that connection between what you know deep down and the

actual beautiful result that you can control is where the magic happens I love that because I think that answers my question definitively which is I have a label for myself as being bad at art therefore I'm always gonna you know be held back in aesthetics I think you're saying no you know unless you literally have some weird blind spots too - what's rubbish like if I smashed her face in and then thought that was okay then I would probably be doomed but actually if I think this one that one you showed us four is a little bit funky then that's good and I can build on that learn mmm yeah and get excited when you feel that way because I mean I do I'm probably going on about it so much but it's it for me I've now got the association when I look at it and I don't like it I feel excited because I know I'm about to discover something if I look at it for long enough and I'm just constantly I look at pictures on the internet and I'm like what is it that why she look alien and then something clicks I change it and then it goes in and I've learned something that I can have that there's now it's now there the connection is made so I hope you found that interesting if you do

decide one day to sculpt your own head please let me know if you want some pointers you can message us or write in the comments and we'll maybe put together some more information if anyone else is interested in doing this kind of thing but I hope you found that interesting and see you next week and please do drop us a like or a subscribe because we love to know that you guys are finding this valuable and for those of you are watching don't forget that this show gets made into a podcast every week as well and that's across all the podcast platforms Spotify and iTunes and all the normal places so please do check us out there as well for your daily commutes thanks for watching take care [Music] you [Music]
